text: Hello, I'm sharing a sigmoid resection for complicated diverticulitis. In this case, the patient presented about a month earlier with an intramural abscess that responded to antibiotics. That was her second known attack in a short period of time, and she elected for surgery. The patient is of a large size, BMI close to forty-seven, had a lot of difficulty keeping small bowel out of the way, which you'll see at different times during the case, and could not really get a good exposure to the IMA, so I decided to do a lateral to medial instead of a medial to lateral. You saw me right there just mark where I presumed a good safe proximal margin would be. I do that because after you mobilize often you lose that visual cue and you could go too short or too long. And so right now I'm just trying to get some freedom so that I can really see what I'm working with. Patient had been asymptomatic for a couple of weeks now. Repeat CAT scans showed significant improvement, so we thought we were good for surgery. Here I'm just showing I'm going to suspend the uterus for a little bit better exposure.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the sigmoid colon, using suction in the left hand to aid visibility. The dissection progresses towards the ureter, visible in ICG mode, with stents providing reassurance of correct anatomical planes. Blunt dissection is used to open natural tissue planes, though the sigmoid colon is adhered to the left adnexa, possibly due to a fistula.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to mobilize the sigmoid colon from the left anterior pelvis, addressing a small abscess in the left adnexa. The surgical field is managed with suction due to oozing, and a sponge is placed to control bleeding. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with the left side elevated, providing exposure for lateral mobilization without needing to mobilize the splenic flexure. The surgeon assesses the diverticulum to determine the extent of resection, avoiding high ligation due to benign disease.

The surgeon creates a window beneath the bowel wall, staying beneath the thickened portion of the mesentery down to the recto sigmoid junction. The small bowel is visible creeping down into the pelvis, influencing the decision to perform a transvaginal extraction due to the patient's body habitus and the thickness of the specimen.

The surgeon uses a stapler to create an anastomosis by joining two staple lines, preferring this method over an open rectal lumen anastomosis. The small bowel is visible deep in the pelvis, complicating the view for extraction. The surgeon places an EEA sizer high towards the cervix to make a posterior opening in the vagina, using a ring clamp for easier specimen extraction compared to transrectal methods. The defect is closed with a 2-0 V-Loc suture, running back and forth.

The surgeon repositions the arm to hold the small bowel over the sacral promontory, preventing it from obstructing the surgical field. The anastomosis is prepared by making openings in the rectum and proximal bowel, joining the staple lines. The tissue between the collotomy and proctotomy is checked for perfusion, with the lips lighting up green, indicating good blood flow. The inner layer is sewn using a three OV lock running stitch towards the patient's left side.

The surgeon brings the stitch to the patient's right and ties the two sutures together. The second outer layer is run back across the top using two PDS sutures. A leak test is performed using a rigid scope. The omentum is placed down in the space adjacent to the anastomosis and vaginal closure. Hemostasis is achieved, and the uterus is repositioned.
